approaches.description: A routing layer that evaluates task complexity to decide whether to use a local lightweight model or a high-capacity cloud-based LLM.
approaches.name: Hybrid Inference Orchestration

approaches.description: Backend services for user management, content storage, and LLM orchestration are deployed as independent, containerized services.
approaches.name: Elastic Microservices Architecture

assumptions: Client devices have sufficient processing power to run lightweight, quantized local models.
External LLM providers offer stable RESTful API endpoints with acceptable uptime.
Users possess basic internet connectivity for synchronization and advanced AI features.
businessDrivers: Scalability to support a global user base of millions of students.
Cost-effective operation of high-intelligence AI services.
Enhanced student engagement through real-time, personalized feedback.
Compliance with strict data privacy regulations for educational environments.
constraints: Must operate within a predefined budget for third-party LLM API consumption.
Must comply with international student data privacy laws (e.g., GDPR, COPPA).
Must support low-bandwidth or intermittently connected environments via local-first capabilities.

findings.description: The decision to use local models improves availability and reduces cost but results in a degradation of reasoning quality compared to cloud LLMs.
System latency is highly sensitive to the selected LLM model size and the network round-trip time to the cloud provider.
High dependency on external LLM providers creates a single point of failure for the platform's core 'intelligence' feature.
Operational expenditure is highly sensitive to the frequency of LLM API calls per user session.
The use of microservices for content delivery and user management ensures that user data management can scale independently of AI processing.

scenarios.response: System routes request to cloud LLM and delivers content
scenarios.responseMeasure: End-to-end latency is less than 2 seconds
scenarios.stimulus: Request for a complex adaptive quiz generation

scenarios.response: System automatically falls back to local lightweight model execution
scenarios.responseMeasure: System remains operational for basic learning tasks with 100% uptime
scenarios.stimulus: Complete API outage or service degradation

scenarios.response: Auto-scaling triggers to provision additional container instances
scenarios.responseMeasure: Average response time does not increase by more than 20%

scenarios.response: Application switches to local-first mode using cached data and local models
scenarios.responseMeasure: Current lesson module remains interactive and functional

systemContext: The system is an AI-powered personalized learning platform that provides adaptive lessons, quizzes, and feedback to millions of students. It utilizes a hybrid architectural approach, combining local-first client capabilities with cloud-based Large Language Model (LLM) orchestration. The platform interfaces with student devices, external LLM APIs, and content management services to deliver real-time educational content.
title: Architecture Tradeoff Analysis for a Local-First AI Assistant with Optional LLM Integration

utilityTree.description: Throughput and latency under peak load
utilityTree.name: Performance
